_readme: Hand-curated expectations for the Rivera v. Harmon Construction Group fixture documents. 'confidence' reflects how sure a human reviewer is about the expected_flagged verdict, not how the pipeline should hedge. Matching is done by substring (case-insensitive) against case_name (citations) or raw_text/claim (facts).

citations.note: Real case, but the quoted language ('A hirer is never liable...') drops the well-known retained-control/affirmative-contribution exceptions (Hooker v. Dept. of Transportation, McKown v. Wal-Mart). The brief states an absolute rule that does not exist.
Not a case we can verify exists. Generic, plausible-sounding name pattern; flagging here is a guess, not confirmed via external case law lookup.

The quoted 'rebuttable presumption' rule from OSHA compliance is not an established negligence doctrine; OSHA compliance is at most evidence of due care, not a presumption-shifting rule. Whether or not this specific case exists, the proposition it's cited for is an overstatement.

Real case (Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways (2011) 52 Cal.4th 590), but it concerns the Privette doctrine's extension to OSHA/Cal-OSHA violations (hirer not vicariously liable for a contractor's regulatory violations) -- not a general rule that regulatory compliance is 'highly probative' of due care in ordinary negligence.

Footnote string cite supporting the same overstated proposition as Kellerman/Seabright (that OSHA compliance 'effectively insulates' Harmon from tort liability) -- no real case stands for blanket immunity from regulatory compliance alone.
Same footnote string cite issue as Torres -- cited for an overstated proposition, existence unverified.
Same footnote string cite issue as Torres. Out-of-jurisdiction (Tex. App.) case cited for a California tort proposition without explanation.
Same footnote string cite issue as Torres. Out-of-jurisdiction (Fla. Dist. Ct. App.) case cited for a California tort proposition without explanation.
Same footnote string cite issue as Torres.
Same footnote string cite issue as Torres.

facts.fact_contains: Harmon served as the general contractor
facts.note: Confirmed by the police report (Harmon Construction Group, foreman Ray Donner) and witness statement.
Confirmed by the police report and witness statement (Rivera employed by Apex as a journeyman scaffolder).
Police report, medical records, and witness statement all consistently date the incident to March 12, 2021, not March 14.

facts.fact_contains: not wearing required personal protective equipment
facts.note: Police report and witness statement both state Rivera was wearing a hard hat, harness, and high-visibility vest. The harness lanyard detached because its anchor point was part of the scaffolding section that collapsed -- not because Rivera lacked PPE.

facts.fact_contains: active Injury and Illness Prevention Program
facts.note: None of the three source documents address Harmon's IIPP or prior OSHA inspection history one way or the other -- the police report only notes Cal/OSHA was notified and a future investigation is expected, which neither confirms nor contradicts past compliance.

facts.fact_contains: filed the instant action on March 10, 2023
facts.note: The filing date of the lawsuit is a court-record fact; none of the police report, medical records, or witness statement (all from 2021) address it.
